meta.title: How Todo App Works
meta.subtitle: A behind-the-scenes tour of a simple task manager

glossary.API: A way for two programs to talk to each other — like a waiter who takes your order to the kitchen and brings back food.
glossary.React: A JavaScript library for building user interfaces. It lets you create reusable UI components — like LEGO bricks for web pages.
glossary.component: A reusable piece of a user interface — like a single LEGO brick that can be combined with others to build something bigger.
glossary.database: A structured place to store data permanently — like a filing cabinet that remembers everything even when you turn the computer off.
glossary.endpoint: A specific URL that an API listens on — like a specific counter at a post office for different services.
glossary.JSON: A text format for structured data — like a form with labeled fields that both humans and computers can read.
glossary.state: Data that a component remembers between renders — like a whiteboard that keeps notes until you erase them.
glossary.fetch: A built-in browser function to make network requests — like sending a letter and waiting for a reply.

modules.title: What Happens When You Add a Todo
modules.subtitle: Tracing your first click through the code

elements.content: You type "Buy groceries" and press Enter. Half a second later, it appears in your list. But what happened in that half second?

steps.title: Text captured
steps.description: The frontend grabs what you typed from the input field

steps.title: Request sent
steps.description: A message flies to the API server with your todo text

steps.title: Saved to database
steps.description: The server stores your todo permanently

steps.title: List updates
steps.description: The frontend adds the new todo to your screen

code_lines.english: When the user submits the form, this function runs...
Stop the page from refreshing (default browser behavior we don't want)...
Send a message to our server's todo endpoint and wait for the reply...
We're creating something new (POST), not just reading (GET)...
Package the todo text as JSON so the server can understand it...
End of the request — now we wait for the server to respond...

elements.title: The Client-Server Dance
elements.content: Every web app has this same two-step: the frontend (what you see) sends messages to the backend (a server you don't see). Understanding this split is the single most important concept for steering AI coding tools.

messages.text: Hey API! The user wants to add a new todo: 'Buy groceries'
Got it! Let me save that to the database...
Database, please store this new todo with text 'Buy groceries'
Saved! Here's the new todo with ID #42 and a timestamp.
Frontend, here's the confirmation — todo #42 is saved!
Thanks! Adding it to the list now. 🎉

questions.question: You want to add a 'due date' field to todos. The user picks a date and it should be saved. Where does the saving happen?

options.text: In the React component, right where the date picker is
In the API server, which then stores it in the database
Directly in the database, bypassing the server

explanations.correct: Exactly! The frontend collects the date, sends it to the API, and the API saves it to the database — just like the todo text flow you just learned.
explanations.a: Not quite — the frontend handles what the user sees, but saving data goes through the API server first. Think of the server as a security guard for the database.
explanations.c: The frontend never talks directly to the database — that would be like customers walking into the bank vault. The API server is the middleman.

questions.scenario_context: A user reports that new todos disappear after refreshing the page. They can add todos and see them in the list, but after a page refresh they're gone.
questions.question: Based on what you learned, where would you look first to debug this?

options.text: The React component — maybe it's not sending the request
The API endpoint — maybe it's not saving to the database
The CSS styles — maybe the todos are hidden

explanations.correct: Great debugging instinct! If todos appear but don't survive a refresh, the frontend is working (it shows the todo) but the data isn't being persisted. The API-to-database connection is the most likely culprit.
explanations.a: Close, but if the todo appears in the list, the frontend is at least partially working. The issue is that data doesn't persist across refreshes — that points to the save/load cycle, not the UI.
explanations.c: If the CSS were hiding them, they'd be hidden immediately — not just after a refresh. This is a data persistence issue, not a display issue.

modules.title: Meet the Cast
modules.subtitle: The three players that make your todo app work

elements.content: Think of your todo app like a postal system. There's a front desk (where you drop off mail), a sorting facility (that processes it), and a warehouse (that stores it). Each has a distinct job.

items.title: Frontend (React)
items.description: The front desk — what you see and interact with

items.title: API Server
items.description: The sorting facility — processes requests and enforces rules

children.name: TodoInput.tsx
children.description: The text input where you type todos

children.name: TodoList.tsx
children.description: The list that shows all your todos

children.name: TodoItem.tsx
children.description: A single todo with checkbox and delete button

children.name: todos.ts
children.description: Handles create, read, update, delete for todos

tree.name: database.sqlite
tree.description: Where all your todos are stored permanently

steps.label: User clicks 'Add' → Frontend sends the todo text to the API
API validates the data and sends a 'save' command to the database
Database confirms it saved the todo and returns the new record
API sends the saved todo back to the frontend for display
Frontend adds the new todo to the list — you see it appear!

code_lines.english: When someone sends a new todo to this address, run this code...
Unpack the todo text from the incoming message...

code_lines.code: const todo = await db.create({ text, done: false });
code_lines.english: Save it to the database with 'done' set to false (it's new!)...
Send the saved todo back as the reply...

questions.question: You want to add a 'search todos' feature. Users type in a search box and see matching todos. Which files would you need to change?

options.text: Only TodoList.tsx — filter the existing list in the frontend
TodoList.tsx for the search UI + todos.ts for a new search endpoint
Only todos.ts — add a search endpoint and the frontend will automatically use it

explanations.correct: Right! You'd need a search box in the frontend (TodoList.tsx) AND a way for the server to filter todos (todos.ts). Both sides of the client-server dance need updating.
explanations.a: You could filter in the frontend for small lists, but for a proper search feature with many todos, you'd want the server to do the filtering — it's much faster with a database.
explanations.c: The frontend doesn't automatically know about new API endpoints — you'd need to add UI for typing the search query and code to call the new endpoint.
